does: This paper develops a two-level deployment framework for safely using potentially untrustworthy large language models in distributed threat settings where isolated failures are not catastrophic. The framework employs micro-protocols that pair an untrusted model with a more capable but extensively tested trusted model for task-level monitoring and control, while a macro-protocol maintains a dynamic credence estimate of the untrusted model's alignment based on accumulated evidence from past interactions. This credence score adaptively selects between safer and riskier micro-protocols, allowing the system to gradually increase utilization of the untrusted model if it demonstrates consistent alignment. The approach extends control evaluations—an adversarial testing methodology—from single-failure scenarios to distributed threat environments where no individual action constitutes overwhelming evidence of misalignment.
connects: This work builds on prior research in adversarial robustness testing and AI safety evaluation, specifically extending the control evaluation framework introduced in previous work that tests deployment strategies against intentionally deceptive models. It responds to the limitation of prior approaches that treat any single failure as unacceptable, which is unrealistic in many real-world deployment contexts. The paper connects to research on sandboxing, monitoring systems, and trust estimation in multi-agent settings, as well as the broader literature on alignment verification and safe deployment of increasingly capable but partially trusted AI systems. It combines ideas from model monitoring, adaptive decision-making, and Bayesian credence updating to address the practical problem of deploying powerful but potentially misaligned models.
enables: This framework opens avenues for deploying more capable untrusted models in production settings where perfect safety guarantees are unattainable but risk can be carefully managed and bounded. It enables future work on refining credence estimation mechanisms, developing more efficient micro-protocols that minimize computational overhead, and extending the approach to multi-model ensembles or hierarchical deployment structures. The distributed threat setting and adaptive framework could inform research on continuous alignment monitoring, dynamic safety thresholds for real-world systems, and strategies for graceful degradation when models show signs of misalignment. It also points toward investigation of optimal macro-protocol design, the interplay between model capability and monitoring difficulty, and how to set appropriate credence thresholds for different risk profiles in application domains where some failure is inevitable.
